page: UpCloud HICPU-8xCPU-12GB is an x86_64 virtual server running on the KVM hypervisor. It is powered by an AMD EPYC 7542 processor with a base clock speed of 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration features 8 vCPUs mapped to 8 physical cores with a single thread per core, utilizing a shared CPU allocation model. The instance is equipped with 12.0 GB of system memory, which establishes a low memory-to-core ratio of 1.5 GB per core. Storage is provided via a bundled 100 GB local SSD, and the network configuration includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No dedicated GPU hardware is included.

Benchmark data indicates a distinct performance profile between single-core and multi-core operations. The stress-ng single-core CPU performance is positioned in the bottom 10%, reflecting the limitations of the shared CPU model and the 2.0 GHz clock speed. Conversely, multi-core execution is more resilient, with the stress-ng multi-core benchmark and the Geekbench multi-core score both aligning with the middle 50% average. Memory bandwidth benchmarks also fall within the average tier, delivering 820.38 GB/sec for cached small-block reads and 41.28 GB/sec for uncached large-block reads.

The primary tradeoff of this configuration is its low memory density relative to its compute cores. With only 1.5 GB of RAM per core, the instance is constrained for memory-intensive applications, as demonstrated by weak performance in medium-model LLM inference. However, it maintains average throughput for lighter operations, achieving 11.58 million operations per second in Redis SET tests and 8.25 GB/sec in static web serving. This server is best suited for multi-threaded, compute-bound workloads with low memory footprints, such as software compilation, text processing, and basic web serving.
description: UpCloud HICPU-8xCPU-12GB is an x86_64 virtual server featuring 8 shared AMD EPYC vCPUs, 12.0 GB of RAM, and a bundled 100 GB local SSD. Operating at 2.0 GHz, the instance offers a low memory-to-core ratio of 1.5 GB per core. Benchmark results show a clear tradeoff, with poor single-core CPU performance but average multi-core and memory bandwidth capabilities. This configuration is best suited for low-memory, multi-threaded workloads such as software compilation and static web serving.

tagline: A shared-core virtual server balancing eight AMD EPYC vCPUs with a compact memory footprint for multi-threaded, compute-focused workloads.
